title: Slab jewellery stand in air-dry clay
subtitle: A flat base with a vertical loop for hanging earrings and necklaces
excerpt: A small jewellery stand built from a rolled clay slab base and a thin clay rod bent into a vertical loop. Air-dry clay sets at room temperature in two to three days, no kiln required. The finished stand is decorative and holds light jewellery: dangle earrings, pendant chains, thin bracelets.
sourceNotes: Slab construction is a foundational hand-building technique with no single attributable origin. V&A and Wikimedia museum open-access collections referenced for general slab-form study. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join. It feels firm like thick leather.
Air-dried but unfired. The clay is pale and uniform in colour. For air-dry clay this is the finished structural state.
Clay diluted with water to a thick brushable consistency. Used as the bonding paste for joining two pieces of clay.

content.text: A jewellery stand keeps dangle earrings, pendant chains, and thin bracelets visible and untangled on the dressing table. This version is two pieces of clay: a flat oval base and a thin rod bent into a vertical loop and joined to the base. Air-dry clay sets at room temperature over two to three days, no kiln required. The finished stand is decorative and holds light jewellery only.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay air-dry for stronger joins

items.name: Two wooden slats (8 mm thick)
items.substitution: Two stacks of three coins each as a guide

content.text: Preparation
Wedge the clay for two or three minutes on the board. Divide into two portions: 200 g for the base and 100 g for the loop. Keep the loop portion wrapped in plastic while working on the base.
Rolling the base slab

Set the two wooden slats parallel on the board, around 15 cm apart. Place the 200 g ball between them and press it flat with the heel of the hand, then roll with the rolling pin from the centre outward. The slats stop the pin from going thinner than 8 mm and keep the slab even. Roll in one direction, then turn the slab a quarter-turn and roll again. The finished slab is around 15 cm by 10 cm.
Cutting the base shape
Trim the slab to an oval around 12 cm by 8 cm with the fettling knife. Save the offcuts under plastic to make
slip

for the join. Round the cut edges by rolling the slab gently between the fingers, or run a damp sponge around the edge to soften it. The finished base sits flat on the dressing table without rocking; check by placing it on a flat surface and pressing gently in the middle.
Rolling and shaping the loop
1. Roll the rod

Roll the 100 g portion into a smooth snake between the palms and the board, 10 mm thick and around 25 cm long. Even pressure rolls an even rod; uneven pressure produces a tapered rod that bends unevenly.
2. Form the loop

Curve the rod gently into an arch around 12 cm tall and 7 cm wide at the widest point. The two ends should sit roughly 4 cm apart, parallel to each other, ready to attach to the base. The arch is the hanging surface. Work slowly; clay rods crack when bent too sharply, so curve in small steps along the length rather than at one fold point.
3. Trim the ends

Trim each end of the rod flat with the fettling knife. The two flat ends will sit on the base. Set the loop on the base in the position it will go, and lightly mark where the two ends touch with the needle tool.
Joining the loop to the base

Score the two marked spots on the base with a cross-hatch of shallow scratches around 1 mm deep, in a circle the size of the rod end. Score the underside of each rod end the same way. Brush slip onto both scored surfaces (slip made from a clay offcut mixed with water to thick cream consistency). Press the two rod ends firmly onto the base. Roll a small rope of clay roughly 3 mm thick, press it around each join as a small fillet, and smooth with a damp finger and then the wooden rib. The fillet adds strength to a join that has to support the cantilever weight of the loop.
Drying

Cover the whole stand loosely with a single layer of plastic for the first twelve hours so the loop and the base dry at the same rate. The two pieces have different thicknesses and uneven drying will pull the join apart. Remove the plastic once the surface feels
leather-hard
and let it finish drying open. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes two to three days at room humidity.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand the base edges and the rod surface lightly with 220-grit sandpaper. Wear a mask: dry clay dust is irritating to breathe. Paint with acrylics or leave plain, then seal with two thin coats of PVA or acrylic varnish brushed on cleanly. A matte varnish reads more natural; a gloss varnish looks more like a glazed ceramic.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof. Keep the stand indoors and away from damp surfaces (so not on the bathroom shelf next to the sink). The sealed stand handles dusting with a soft cloth. Hang light jewellery only: dangle earrings, pendant chains, thin bracelets. Heavy chains or watches will gradually stress the rod-to-base join.
Variations
Stacked rings instead of an arch: roll three small clay rings (15 mm diameter), join them stacked on top of each other to a vertical rod attached to the base. Each ring holds a different chain.
Stamp texture into the base before cutting: press a piece of lace, a coarse fabric, or a leaf onto the soft slab and roll over with the pin. The texture stays after the slab firms.
Add a small ring dish to the base: press a 30 g ball of clay into a flat disc and pinch up a 5 mm wall to make a shallow dish before the slab base sets. Score and slip the join.

items.symptom: Loop pops off the base while drying
items.cause: Score-and-slip join too shallow or slip too dry
items.fix: Re-attach with deeper scoring (1 mm) and thicker slip; add a clay fillet around the join for extra support

items.symptom: Loop cracks at the highest point of the arch
items.cause: Rod bent too sharply at one point instead of in a smooth curve
items.fix: On the next attempt, curve in small steps along the whole length; press fresh slip into the crack and dry under plastic

items.symptom: Base warps as it dries (lifts at the edges)
items.cause: Bottom of the base dried faster than the top
items.fix: Dry the base under plastic for the first twelve hours and turn it over once a day during drying

items.symptom: Stand topples when an earring is hung on the loop
items.cause: Base is too small or too light to balance the loop's cantilever
items.fix: Scale up to a 14 cm by 10 cm base, or add a small pinched ring dish to the base front for ballast
